title: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far
display_title: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far
wikitext:
[]: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far? Post by Chowwow » Fri Apr 25, 2008 12:56 am We all know that Evangelion happens to be a somewhat popular property, and so this anime has spawned a great legion of fans and otaku who buy into Gainax's whoring of the franchise. We have all heard about the irony of this reality as this was the opposite of what Anno wanted to depict all along - so I won't be repeating it. My question is: has it gone too far? We all have our otaku moments here (we're the EvaGeeks, after all), but has anyone seen or done anything where you just can't help but to say "This has gone too far..."? Here is account of my experience: Back during my early days at IMDB.com, I was pitching a few NGE ideas in the forums when I met a user there. To protect the innocent, I will call him Toji – who for the record, was nothing like Toji of Evangelion fame at all. Toji was, as far as I could see, a very intelligent and well-versed NGE fan. He always had something useful and smart to contribute to threads and he was an early supporter for some of my ideas. One day, there was a thread in the boards about a certain aqua-haired girl we all know and fap to. Many people, including Toji, gushed about her: how she was so perfect and beautiful, and how they all wished they could meet a girl like her, etc. I, being the bombastic, holier-than-thou, know-it-all who types too damn much, decided it would be smart to make a post about my concerns about the otaku scene and the consumption of hentai staring the under-aged anime females of NGE. I made comments how people were shutting themselves from reality because of this, reducing themselves into real-life Shinjis, and how much ire Anno had for NGE obsession, etc. Contrary to what one may expect from otaku, rather than suffering a battery of insults and general misery, what I got instead was a somewhat disorganized and rambling response from none other than Toji. To paraphrase from blurred memory: he said that loving Rei wasn’t that much of a deal, that people shouldn’t be forced to dislike the things that they love by others, etc. Fair enough, so for a while I let the issue die down. It was about that time that I noticed something very strange: Toji’s posts started to get shorter, less informative, and more defensive and he started taking some issues personally. He started to draw a few references to some group of people that he could only refer to as “they” or “them”. Even more puzzling, he started harassing other users and keeping a “blacklist” of people that he had trouble with for public viewing. One day, I approached him to join me in a project in the hope that he could further lend his insight. Instead, what I got was a two page e-mail about why he couldn’t join me, and what was bothering him that prevented him from doing so. In short, Toji felt that my comments had removed the affections that he once had for Rei, revealing them to be perverse and shameful things. He said that Rei was a source of inspiration and hope for him in the past, but now he could no longer find comfort in her – all while seeming to imply that I was the root cause of his disillusionment. He complained of lack of sleep and inability to focus on writing, as well as being unable to work on his own creative projects. Furthermore, he said he was being oppressed and attacked from all sides by something he later called the “homosexual conspiracy”, members of which I could only assume were the “them” he previously mentioned. You see, there was another element to his distress: Toji felt that gays and lesbians were attempting to push their lifestyle on to him, possibly to “convert” him. He felt that homosexuals were being too up-front with their lifestyle and beliefs, and that now they were being given favoritism by the public. Toji thought that gays were being held as sacred people in the world and that there was an opinion that in order to be enlightened and open-minded in the modern age, one had to be gay. He said that he saw this everywhere, including as a subliminal message in one line of my old signature, “Dare to think, Dare to be different!” To explain, he interpreted the line as, in order to think freely, one had to be different – such as to be gay. He even accused some (if not all) IMDB users and the site itself as being proponents of the conspiracy – including individuals whom we both considered friends. He boycotted certain media pieces, such as South Park, because they spread some kind of pro-gay dogma. He was clearly upset about this, as his later e-mails to me went at length about his insecurities, thoughts and suspicions. It was clear that this was a fairly long-standing insecurity that was starting to get out of hand in real life. He didn’t want to be gay, and he wanted to have a normal life with a normal wife and future. However, it should be interesting to note that he was somewhat more tolerant of “hot” lesbians… Anyway, Toji felt that Rei was his beacon of hope against the “homosexual conspiracy”, as if his admiration for Rei protected him from all of this. Rei seemed to uphold a view of women, purity, and/or beauty that he wanted to find in the world. She seemed to imply to him that real love could be found in the vastness of the Earth, and was cited as a source of his creative strength and energy. Rei was proof of his heterosexual orientation, and possibly as a result, shielded him from the battery of assaults from the gay media. Toji mentioned several times that he wanted to find a girl who was like Rei one day and fall in love with her, so that he may walk away from this in happiness. However, because she was now “gone” from his life, none of this seemed possible. It seemed to him that he would be forced into homosexuality, and thus, never find the love and happiness he truly desired. He even went to a psychologist for counseling – one who reportedly assured Toji that he was “not gay” and happened to be in the opinion that homosexuality should not have been eliminated from the list of mental disorders by the American Psychological Association. I, being the kind-hearted and outstanding dumbass I was and still am, worked hard to encourage him via e-mail using my composition skill and cliché “keep-it-real-dawg” philosophy. I gave him the typical rebuttals, denials, and alternate viewpoints that I’m sure would be made apparent by now. I tried to calm him down off the soap box and get him to find confidence in himself against his problems. I used epic lines such as: “Find the strength to live for yourself and others, and use the fire in your soul to burn the sky!” (Note that this was before Gurren Lagann’s time). Or in response to him never finding a real-life Rei in his life, and settling for an “average” girl instead – if at all: “You can still find that special someone who is like Rei, who rivals Rei, or even surpasses Rei, if you are willing to get close enough. Don't look for Rei, look for the right one who will accept you and Rei may find you. Never settle for "average", open your heart to her and she will do the same - that is love.” As you could tell, I was very committed to maintaining this relationship, so that Toji and I could create something together and rule the world – or something… Then I found out that Toji was replacing his hours posting in IMDB with hours spent on a white supremacist forum, evidently to join them in their anti-gay discussions and the degradation of society because of it. I also caught wind that he had made some fan fiction of Rei in – let’s just say – compromising positions. Finally, it became clear to me that things were not getting any better: he still held his insecure beliefs about gays, his longing for Rei continued, and my appeals for sanity went unanswered. My helpful and desperate drive to find forgiveness and to give confidence gave way to annoyed rage: I could not associate with people who could be this intolerant and insensitive for this long. After a few more warnings and sporadic communications, we finally dropped contact from each other. Since then, Toji seems to have dropped off the internet entirely, hopefully getting better in his own terms in real life. I refuse to call this situation pathetic in anyway, but I feel saddened by this course of events: I may have lost a friend but the world may have lost a great mind. And I have to wonder: was all of this really about Rei in the end? Did his fandom save him from his problems – for a time? Or did it exacerbate them? Or was I to blame for being too harsh and trying to “help”?

[]: Post by BobBQ » Fri Apr 25, 2008 1:41 am Humans are scum, film at eleven. On a serious note, my impression is that this guy had pretty significant issues unrelated to Eva and that these issues would have messed him up regardless of whether he was an Eva fan.

[]: Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Apr 25, 2008 2:05 am Yeah, he sounded pretty badly messed up. And if not Rei, he'd have found someone else to fetishize. He was essentially grabbing at strings in the middle of his insecurity. His fandom was more of an outlet...and considering his issues, being a fan wouldn't matter in the least.

[]: Post by TheAyanamiOtaku » Fri Apr 25, 2008 2:52 am you can't find a "Rei", because the kind of angels that exist in the Eva Universe don't exist here. I can understand where this Touji guy is coming from, Rei is partially my inspiration too. But everyone has to realize that there is no one "perfect gal". You can get close. Unless you're Korben Dallas, and that's a special case. Nobody is forcing homosexuality. It's just that many more people are being increasingly accepting, NOT homosexual. That's what Touji doesn't understand, among other things. Lol, I have a feeling that he's had homosexual feelings before, and that he doesn't want to admit to them for fear of realizing that he may be homosexual. Like you said earlier, it wasn't really about Rei. It was about what Toji thought what Rei symbolized, and that was chastity and innocence and all that jazz, and that's not exactly true. It just seems that way, but you really need to look further. She has longing and anger and frustration, but she doesn't show it like anyone else does. Most likely, this guy's depressed from the lack of purpose that he has in his real life, and it might be too late for him to change that. So, unconsciously, he battles for all that is good and pure on the internet, because it seemingly gives him purpose, and HOPE. It is evident when he complains of lack of sleep and yada yada. Those are self inflicted...unless he's at like, Guantanamo or something. It takes balls to look into blackness and walk into it because you know it's the only way to reach the end of the tunnel. I've had dreams with Rei, fantasies...dirty and WAFF, but there's a blurry line between going too far and going with one's passion. Rei IS beautiful, but I have come to realize that fapping to her is not appreciating her entire beauty. Fapping to her is taking for her face value and rolling with it. So I took a hiatus from this and tried to analyze more deeply into Rei's personality....and that's when I found you guys. But the point is, there is so much more to enjoy about Rei then simply her face, her body, her breasts, and her smile...it's what's inside...to me. She isn't perfect, and Anno realizes that there isn't such a thing, but it's like looking at a weathered statue... You still know she's beautiful. Well, I hope I never meet the guy, or else he might try and kill me...or label me as a they or them or whatever...I have a feeling that everyone on this Earth, because of our intelligence and stuff, is at least somewhat bisexual. Hope I helped in trying to understand this Toji dude.

[]: Post by Asuka's Plug Suit » Fri Apr 25, 2008 3:32 am Unfortunately, there are whackjobs...and sometimes they like the same things we like. I wouldn't hold him up as an "average" Eva fan... But to five my answer to the question in your title, I think Eva fandom went too far the night a bunch of "fans" defaced the Gainax building...

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Apr 25, 2008 4:58 am To paraphrase a line from V for Vendetta: "Artists use lies to tell the truth, the audience uses that lie to cover up their own truths." So many call it escapism, but the problem goes much deeper than that. Many like Toji are desperately looking for something to latch onto like a crutch when everything around you and inside you seems broken and crippled. Or as Frank McConnell wrote in the intro to Sandman Vol. 9: "Art isn't order out of chaos, but the dream of order out of a sense of chaos". I think that's what so many find or can find in any given work. In this particular case I think one is inclined to ask "Why Rei from NGE?". Well, what is Rei? This seemingly emotionless object of a female - this near perfect portrayal of the male Anima. She's a subservient being that works equally well as mother, lover, and goddess. When someone latches onto a concept like her in order to make sense of their world I think there's several logics that could be inferred from it. As to answer the OP question directly, I don't think this particular case has anything to do with taking NGE too far. It has to do with an individual finding something in fiction that makes their reality make sense. That's not a knock on the work or its fandom, it's a very personal problem. I also don't know if your post on the topic triggered his disillusionment or not. It's possible, but I'd suggest that it was only an external statement from someone else of what he'd known all along inside and didn't want to admit. As bizarre as it sounds, people can even come to revelations about themselves and their life over the internet. I think the impossible question is whether or not the change is healthy or not for him. Again, some people construct these personal fantasies out of absolute necessity. Sometimes it's only the lies that keep us sane. I'm not saying he's insane now, but he's probably just finding this adjustment to a hard and rocky road tough to navigate without that support cane.

[]: Post by Iron Rooster » Fri Apr 25, 2008 12:31 pm The guy sounds like one of the people who needs to talk to a psychologist. His problems were really deep already before you met him. I could see where he gets his ideas, but he totally went about his problems the wrong way. I hope those white supremacist guys didn't mess him up too much more. People like that seem to only feel hate for those unlike them. Sorry for your loss.

[]: Post by Grand Duke of Yashima » Fri Apr 25, 2008 6:50 pm (No double-posting, please. Use the edit button instead) In regards to "Toji": I think this guy was generally suffering from Lonely Otaku Syndrome (or what Anno calls his "self-imposed autism"). Basically you have a fairly intelligent shut-in who fixates on an anime character as a substitute for an actual relationship. In his case, he craved purity in an unpure world, and Rei symbolized that to him. I didn't have to be Rei, it could have been Belldandy, or Valentine from Gankutsuou or whatever, but he felt "dirty" and wanted a partner who could maintain his uprightness. I'm not sure how the whole "gay conspiracy" thing works into this, and I'm not so sure it matters that much. Suffice to say, an anime character is not going to reinforce your own personality defects, though they may help you understand them. Certainly, one of the big themes of EVA is that we're defined by our weaknesses. I speak from personal experience from this point. When I got into anime, I was really very much of a shut-in, and could find myself easily getting fixated on female characters. It took some getting out of the shell to realize that A) real people are (usually) not like anime characters but B) on most days, they're fun anyway. For me, the cure was taking Japanese in night school. Once I did that, it was interacting with real people, not just a video display, and it opened a whole world to me. I think that "Toji" could stand for some of that kind of therapy, too. Not that I don't think that Rei is pretty cute, of course....

[]: Re: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far? Post by NAveryW » Fri Apr 25, 2008 7:44 pm Chowwow wrote: Rei seemed to uphold a view of women, purity, and/or beauty that he wanted to find in the world. She seemed to imply to him that real love could be found in the vastness of the Earth, and was cited as a source of his creative strength and energy.

[]: Post by TheAyanamiOtaku » Fri Apr 25, 2008 9:31 pm Perception is not the same as reality, and reality is not the absolute truth.

[]: Re: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far? Post by universalperson » Sat Apr 26, 2008 1:26 pm This seems to be a typical case of what I can only think of as "online schizophrenia"...a rational person suddenly seeming to "snap" and go down a slippery slope. He also reminds me of my homophobic phase. Ah, good times, good times (not really).

[]: Post by NAveryW » Sat Apr 26, 2008 2:42 pm TheAyanamiOtaku wrote: reality is not the absolute truth.

[]: Post by Indigo Arcangel » Sat Apr 26, 2008 4:38 pm Just popping in to say...this is probably the most interesting thread that I've read in a long while. I'll probably post more later, but it's too late at the moment and I'm too tired. What I will say tho' is that this is the first time I've really heard a detailed account of someone who's become infatuated with a fictional character, and I find it very compelling - mainly because I went through a similar experience about two years ago, when I was 14. D.N.Angel was the first manga that I 'properly' read. Two years back, I was a lot worse in the autism and sociability department, and the fact that anime had turned me into a scretive romantic didn't help my insecurities. The character of Riku turned into a focus of actual physical infatuation for me as I projected myself onto the main character of Daisuke - A tendency I share between DNANGEL and Eva. To say that I felt similar to this guy would not be unfounded, and it feels quite profound - in both our cases - that a fictional character turned into a sort of melodramatic rock for us to clutch onto.

[]: Post by THE Hal E. Burton 9000 » Sat Apr 26, 2008 8:54 pm I want to write up MOAR on this when I have the time and wherewithall, but for now I'll just sum up a few main points I don't know what "Toji's" problems are, frankly I don't REALLY want to know he just might have some sort of mental or personality disorder, and been raised in an environment that was highly "pious" which fear and self-loathing were "the order of things", possibly even abused in a highly personal or sexual way he also may find his fixation on Rei and raging homphobia as "his way" of dealing with his idiosyncric "state" I would say to not blame yourself AT ALL for "Toji", he's made some choices in life that's he is obviously struggling with, some of those choices he might not have had "a say in"

[]: Post by Dartz » Sat Apr 26, 2008 9:43 pm Some people take things way to far... But such is the way with life. We're all looking for some quick escape from our dull and boring lives. Some people find it in vacuous celebrity watching, others find it in vacuous celebrity shooting and even others find it in the myriad of televisions fandoms floating around out there. This isn't anything that unusual... in another life that same person might become a stalker, or an obsessive. I suppose because the subject of their obsession exists only on a few hundred thousand cells...and a a fucktonne of cheap merchandising... is what makes it in anyway strange. To the obsessive, even fictional characters can seem as real as their own mother....it doesn't matter if they're animated, or a live action TV show. Heck, I've gotten so deep into the heads of some characters trying to write fanfics involving them, it leaves a lingering feeling that maybe....I am them. Not strong, but it is a thought that has occurred to me more than once. I mean, given the content of the series...my whole life to this point could just be an Illusion. I could go to bed tonight, and wake up on a beach. What a strange idea...that NGE could be instrumentalities way of trying to remind me of my own personal identity. But, it is something that has occured to me.... I even used it/am using it... as a point in a story. That's not an attempt at a joke, or making light of the situation, like I would normally would, I'm trying to be serious about this. I doesn't help matters that I can sometimes find several aspects of Shinji's personality in myself.. That is sometimes unsettling, especially given my tendancy to put myself into a characters shoes and wonder how I would do in their place. Am I in anyway stranger than Toji?.... I don't know. I'm regarded as a 'weird' person by people who know me.... it's a label I'm perversly proud of.... but at the same time, I wonder how weird am I really? Looking at a different band in the spectrum of obsessiveness is making me uncomfortable. Again, because of my minds tendancy to try place itself in another persons shoes, I wonder how close I am...or how outright different I am to Toji? What would I do if I saw Rei Ayanami waiting for a train at Connolly station Monday morning on my Way to work? What would I do if I wake up tomorrow morning on a coral beach, staring up at a glistening night sky, a streak of red slashing through the stars and suddenly realised that none of you or myself even existed? It's strange... I do in part hope that happens. I would like to see what happens next. I suppose I'm rambling a bit off topic.... but it's 3:40 am. and I'm insomniac today. You can forgive me that I hope.

[]: Post by Steve » Sat Apr 26, 2008 10:10 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: To paraphrase a line from V for Vendetta: "Artists use lies to tell the truth, the audience uses that lie to cover up their own truths."

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 27, 2008 5:38 am Watched a film just yesterday called Close-Up by Iranian director Abbas Kiarostami and it made me think of this thread. The film is a part documentary, part recreation (with the real people involved) account of a man who deceived a family for several weeks under the pretense that he was Iranian director Mohsen Makhmalbaf. He carried on this charade until he was eventually arrested and brought to trial for fraud. At the trial it was repeatedly asked why he impersonated the director. He talked extensively about how so strongly he related with the character in Makhmalbaf's film "The Cyclist", how he felt so strongly about artists who depicted people's suffering, and about how nice it was to be thought of as someone - like a director - who people would listen to and look up. It was a fascinating film with so many thematic layers about how fiction influences our lives. Here is this man, who was so profoundly moved by an artist and one of his works that he took on the persona of that person. Not for money, or for fame, but... out of respect? Out of want of respect? And in doing so influenced another artist to create a film about a true life story of a man who used art to influence his life.... I think it speaks volumes of people's need, or atleast desperate want for art and fiction. Of the depths that it touches and moves us and how it makes us grapple with truths that sometimes we'd rather not. How sometimes we turn fiction into our reality, or use fiction to cover up our actual reality. Anyway, it gives you a lot to think about.

[]: Post by mrpanda » Sun Apr 27, 2008 6:15 am Ive suffered with obsession before, mostly due to my AS, and no its not good but on the other hand its some peoples way of living and if you take that away what have they got left. Sometimes its better to just leave someone like that as breaking them out of their closed world can often be very damaging.

[]: Post by Zuggy » Sun Apr 27, 2008 10:51 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: To paraphrase a line from V for Vendetta: "Artists use lies to tell the truth, the audience uses that lie to cover up their own truths."

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 27, 2008 12:00 pm Zuggy wrote: Seems exactly the same as Picasso famously said with an extra bit stuck on the end.

We have all heard about the irony of this reality as this was the opposite of what Anno wanted to depict all along - so I won't be repeating it. My question is: has it gone too far? We all have our otaku moments here &#4

Don't look for Rei, look for the right one who will accept you and Rei may find you. Never settle for "average", open your heart to her and she will do the same - that is love.” As you could tell, I was very committed to maintaining this relationship, so that Toji and I could create something together and rule the world – or something… Then I found out that Toji was replacing his hours posting in IMDB with hours spent on a white supremacist forum, evidently to join them in their anti-gay discussions and the degradation of society because of it. I also caught wind that he had made some fan fiction of Rei in – let’s just say – compromising positions. Finally, it became clear to me that things were not getting any better: he still held his insecure beliefs about gays, his longing for Rei continued, and my appeals for sanity went unanswered. My helpful and desperate drive to find forgiveness and to give confidence gave way to annoyed rage: I could not associate with people who could be this intolerant and insensitive for this long. After a few more warnings and sporadic communications, we finally dropped contact from each other. Since then, Toji seems to have dropped off the internet entirely, hopefully getting better in his own terms in real life. I refuse to call this situation pathetic in anyway, but I feel saddened by this course of events: I may have lost a friend but the world may have lost a great mind. And I have to wonder: was all of this really about Rei in the end? Did his fandom save him from his problems – for a time? Or did it exacerbate them? Or was I to blame for being too harsh and trying to “help”?

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Apr 25, 2008 4:58 am To paraphrase a line from V for Vendetta: "Artists use lies to tell the truth, the audience uses that lie to cover up their own truths." So many call it escapism, but the problem goes much deeper than that. Many like Toji are desperately looking for something to latch onto like a crutch when everything around you and inside you seems broken and crippled. Or as

Article: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far
 their own truths." So many call it escapism, but the problem goes much deeper than that. Many like Toji are desperately looking for something to latch onto like a crutch when everything around you and inside you seems broken and crippled. Or as Frank McConnell wrote in the intro to Sandman Vol. 9: "Art isn't order out of chaos, but the dream of order out of a sense of chaos". I think that's what so many find or can find in any given work. In this particular case I think one is inclined to ask "Why Rei from NGE?". Well, what is Rei? This seemingly emotionless object of a female - this near perfect portrayal of the male Anima. She's a subservient being that works equally well as mother, lover, and goddess. When someone latches onto a concept like her in order to make sense of their world I think there's several logics that could be inferred from it. As to answer the OP question directly, I don't think this particular case has anything to do with taking NGE too far. It has to do with an individual finding something in fiction that makes their reality make sense. That's not a knock on the work or its fandom, it's a very personal problem. I also don't know if your post on the topic triggered his disillusionment or not. It's possible, but I'd suggest that it was only an external statement from someone else of what he'd known all along inside and didn't want to admit. As bizarre as it sounds, people can even come to revelations about themselves and their life over the internet. I think the impossible question is whether or not the change is healthy or not for him. Again, some people construct these personal fantasies out of absolute necessity. Sometimes it's only the lies that keep us sane. I'm not saying he's insane now, but he's probably just finding this adjustment to a hard and rocky road tough to navigate without that support cane.

[]: Post by Grand Duke of Yashima » Fri Apr 25, 2008 6:50 pm (No double-posting, please. Use the edit button instead) In regards to "Toji": I think this guy was generally suffering from Lonely Otaku Syndrome (or what Anno calls his "self-imposed autism"). Basically you have a fairly intelligent shut-in who fixates on an anime character as a substitute for an actual relationship. In his case, he craved purity in an unpure world, and Rei symbolized that to him. I didn't have to be Rei, it could have been Belldandy, or Valentine from Gankutsuou or whatever, but he felt "dirty" and wanted a partner who could maintain his uprightness. I'm not sure how the whole "gay conspiracy" thing works into this, and I'm not so sure it matters that much. Suffice to say, an anime character is not going to reinforce your own personality defects, though they may help you understand them. Certainly, one of the big themes of EVA is that we're defined by our weaknesses. I speak from personal experience from this point. When I got into anime, I was really very much of a shut-in, and could find myself easily getting fixated on female characters. It took some getting out of the shell to realize that A) real people are (usually) not like anime characters but B) on most days, they're fun anyway. For me, the cure was taking Japanese in night school. Once I did that, it was interacting with real people, not just a video display, and it opened a whole world to me. I think that "Toji" could stand for some of that kind of therapy, too. Not that I don't think that Rei is pretty cute, of course....

[]: Post by Dartz » Sat Apr 26, 2008 9:43 pm Some people take things way to far... But such is the way with life. We're all looking for some quick escape from our dull and boring lives. Some people find it in vacuous celebrity watching, others find it in vacuous celebrity shooting and even others find it in the myriad of televisions fandoms floating around out there. This isn't anything that unusual... in another life that same person might become a stalker, or an obsessive. I suppose because the subject of their obsession exists only on a few hundred thousand cells...and a a fucktonne of cheap merchandising... is what makes it in anyway strange. To the obsessive, even fictional characters can seem as real as their own mother....it doesn't matter if they're animated, or a live action TV show. Heck, I've gotten so deep into the heads of some characters trying to write fanfics involving them, it leaves a lingering feeling that maybe....I am them. Not strong, but it is a thought that has occurred to me more than once. I mean, given the content of the series...my whole life to this point could just be an Illusion. I could go to bed tonight, and wake up on a beach. What a strange idea...that NGE could be instrumentalities way of trying to remind me of my own personal identity. But, it is something that has occured to me....

Article: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far
. I could go to bed tonight, and wake up on a beach. What a strange idea...that NGE could be instrumentalities way of trying to remind me of my own personal identity. But, it is something that has occured to me.... I even used it/am using it... as a point in a story. That's not an attempt at a joke, or making light of the situation, like I would normally would, I'm trying to be serious about this. I doesn't help matters that I can sometimes find several aspects of Shinji's personality in myself.. That is sometimes unsettling, especially given my tendancy to put myself into a characters shoes and wonder how I would do in their place. Am I in anyway stranger than Toji?.... I don't know. I'm regarded as a 'weird' person by people who know me.... it's a label I'm perversly proud of.... but at the same time, I wonder how weird am I really? Looking at a different band in the spectrum of obsessiveness is making me uncomfortable. Again, because of my minds tendancy to try place itself in another persons shoes, I wonder how close I am...or how outright different I am to Toji? What would I do if I saw Rei Ayanami waiting for a train at Connolly station Monday morning on my Way to work? What would I do if I wake up tomorrow morning on a coral beach, staring up at a glistening night sky, a streak of red slashing through the stars and suddenly realised that none of you or myself even existed? It's strange... I do in part hope that happens. I would like to see what happens next. I suppose I'm rambling a bit off topic.... but it's 3:40 am. and I'm insomniac today. You can forgive me that I hope.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sun Apr 27, 2008 5:38 am Watched a film just yesterday called Close-Up by Iranian director Abbas Kiarostami and it made me think of this thread. The film is a part documentary, part recreation (with the real people involved) account of a man who deceived a family for

Article: Has Evangelion fandom gone too far
ed a film just yesterday called Close-Up by Iranian director Abbas Kiarostami and it made me think of this thread. The film is a part documentary, part recreation (with the real people involved) account of a man who deceived a family for several weeks under the pretense that he was Iranian director Mohsen Makhmalbaf. He carried on this charade until he was eventually arrested and brought to trial for fraud. At the trial it was repeatedly asked why he impersonated the director. He talked extensively about how so strongly he related with the character in Makhmalbaf's film "The Cyclist", how he felt so strongly about artists who depicted people's suffering, and about how nice it was to be thought of as someone - like a director - who people would listen to and look up. It was a fascinating film with so many thematic layers about how fiction influences our lives. Here is this man, who was so profoundly moved by an artist and one of his works that he took on the persona of that person. Not for money, or for fame, but... out of respect? Out of want of respect? And in doing so influenced another artist to create a film about a true life story of a man who used art to influence his life.... I think it speaks volumes of people's need, or atleast desperate want for art and fiction. Of the depths that it touches and moves us and how it makes us grapple with truths that sometimes we'd rather not. How sometimes we turn fiction into our reality, or use fiction to cover up our actual reality. Anyway, it gives you a lot to think about.
